title: Sweet potato soup
subtitle: Creamy, spiced, and finished with coconut
excerpt: Sweet potatoes blended with coconut milk, fresh ginger, and a little chilli into a vivid orange soup. Fast, naturally thick, and reliably good. Ready in 35 minutes from scratch.
sourceNotes: Sweet potato soup blended with spices and coconut milk became a British supermarket and home-cooking standard from the 2000s onwards. The combination draws on Caribbean, South Asian, and African cooking traditions without being specific to any one.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before adding coconut milk. Keeps for up to three months. Add coconut milk when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. An excellent batch-cook soup.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to three days ahead. Thickens on standing; thin with water or stock.

glossaryTerms.definition: The orange-fleshed variety of sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas), now the dominant type sold in British supermarkets. The orange flesh comes from beta-carotene and gives blended soups a vivid colour. Do not substitute with white-fleshed sweet potato.

content.text: Sweet potato soup is fast, requires no technique, and produces a vivid

result that is difficult to improve on. The coconut milk rounds the sweetness and adds richness; the fresh ginger and chilli cut through it. Blend until very smooth ,  the texture is one of the things that makes this soup satisfying.

Heat {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-fresh}} of grated ginger, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, and {{cumin-ground}} of cumin. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the {{sweet-potato}} of diced sweet potato and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 to 18 minutes until the sweet potato is completely tender.
Blend until very smooth. Stir in {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and the juice of {{lime}} lime. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls. Scatter coriander leaves over each bowl if using.

items.symptom: Soup is very thick and dense
items.cause: Sweet potato is starchy and produces a very thick purée.
items.fix: Add extra hot stock or water after blending until the consistency pours easily. The soup should be thick but still flow from a ladle.

items.cause: Sweet potatoes vary greatly in sweetness; some are very sugary.
items.fix: Add more lime juice and a pinch of extra chilli. The lime is essential ,  it lifts and balances the sweetness. More ginger also helps.

items.symptom: Coconut milk has split into oil and solids
items.cause: The coconut milk was added to a boiling soup rather than a simmering one.
items.fix: Add coconut milk off the heat or at a gentle simmer. Give the can a good shake before opening. Full-fat coconut milk behaves better than light.

items.symptom: Colour is pale rather than vivid orange
items.cause: White-fleshed sweet potatoes were used by mistake.
items.fix: Look for sweet potatoes with orange or copper-coloured skin ,  the flesh inside will be orange. White-fleshed varieties (sometimes called boniato) are a different product.

content.text: Sweet potato with coconut and spices appears across Caribbean, South Asian, and West African cooking. In Britain this combination arrived as a supermarket soup in the 2000s. Sweet potatoes moved from specialist shops to year-round supermarket staples, and the homemade version followed. It is now one of the most-made soups in British kitchens.
